text: Two ellipses are marked over the umbilicus, and a stab wound is made at the inferior crease using an #11 blade. The surgeon inserts a Veress needle into the incision, lifting the abdominal wall and orienting it towards the anus. After confirming placement with irrigation, suction, and hanging drop tests, the insufflator tubing is connected, and insufflation begins. Once the preset pressure is reached, the Veress needle is removed, and the incision is enlarged. A mosquito forceps is used to dilate the channel, followed by the insertion of the primary trocar using a screwing motion.

The surgeon introduces a telescope through the trocar placed in the left hypochondrium due to extensive adhesions. Using a harmonic device, adhesiolysis is performed to separate the left lobe of the liver from the stomach and costal margins. The falciform ligament and right lobe of the liver are visible, but the gallbladder is obscured by adhesions.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis using a harmonic device to separate adhesions between the stomach, gallbladder, and omentum. The adhesions are carefully dissected from the inferior margin of the liver, ensuring the harmonic's silicone tip is oriented away from the gallbladder to prevent puncture.

The gallbladder is visibly impacted with a large stone measuring approximately 4.6 to 8 centimeters. Adhesions around the fundus region have been mostly separated. The surgeon uses the left hand to hold the omentum while the right hand separates remaining adhesions in the Calot's triangle. The stomach is visible below the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a fundus clasp to hold the gallbladder fundus and retract it towards the right shoulder, facilitating the separation of adhesions between the duodenum and stomach.

The surgeon identifies a band between the stomach and the cystic pedicle, pushing it down to reveal the common bile duct (CBD) and duodenum below. Anteromedial traction is applied to the Hartmann's pouch, facilitating the opening of the posterior peritoneum above the Rouviere's sulcus. The gallbladder is partially separated from the liver, with additional adhesions being dissected to ease posterior peritoneum dissection. The stomach is further mobilized downward, and the peritoneum between the gallbladder and liver is separated using anteromedial traction.

The surgeon applies anteromedial traction to separate the posterior peritoneum above the roviate sulcus, then shifts to anterolateral traction to separate the peritoneum and expose one-third of the gallbladder body. The posterior and anterior windows are created, revealing the inferior surface of the liver and aiding in the definition of Calot's triangle. The peritoneum over the cystic pedicle is separated, skeletonizing the cystic duct and artery, with the common bile duct visible below.

The surgeon forms the anterior window by separating the peritoneum over the cystic pedicle using a Maryland dissector, enlarging the window to 2.2 centimeters. The harmonic device is used to skeletonize the visceral peritoneum over the cystic pedicle. The posterior window is visible between the artery and the liver, while the anterior window is between the artery and duct. An extracorporeal Misrach knot is fed into the window, tied outside, and secured with a knot pusher near the common bile duct (CBD). The knot is cut, and a clip is applied.

A titanium clip is applied to the gallbladder and another to the cystic artery, ensuring both jaws of the clip are visible. The harmonic device is used to cut the artery, keeping the vibrating jaw under vision. The cystic pedicle is separated, and the knot securing the cystic duct is visible. Anteromedial and anterolateral traction is used to separate the gallbladder from the liver, cutting the peritoneal shoulders and separating areolar tissue. A large stone is observed, and an endobag is recommended for use.

The gallbladder is removed and placed into a commercially available endo bag. The surgeon opens the mouth of the endo bag and introduces the gallbladder, which contains a single large stone. Using ovum forceps, the stone is crushed into smaller pieces to facilitate removal. The gallbladder and stone fragments are extracted through the epigastric port, which is then closed with a suture.
